Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings after work, but being 27 and juggling everything, it rarely went according to plan. I learn best by listening, so I'd often have lectures playing while doing other things around my apartment. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything—it really depended on my mental state and what else was going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and how anxious I was feeling. When assignments were due, I'd cram in study sessions, but otherwise I'd drift for days without touching course materials. I tried to establish regular times, but my anxiety would sometimes make it impossible to focus even when I sat down to study. The inconsistency frustrated me, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification from a classmate in our peer support group. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't accomplish much. My study group would message me asking about discussion posts or upcoming tasks, and that social pressure was honestly what got me logging in most often. Without those nudges, I might have drifted even more.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine along with coffee—I'd scroll through announcements and see what my peers were posting. The peer support intervention really helped with this because I felt accountable to actual people, not just a system. But that routine would break whenever my stress levels spiked, and then I'd avoid logging in for days because even looking at the platform made me anxious.
Q5: The recorded lectures were my lifeline since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times, pause, rewind—it suited how my brain processes information. The discussion forums were also valuable because connecting with peers made the content feel less overwhelming. When classmates explained concepts in their own words, it often clicked better than the formal materials.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. My eyes would glaze over trying to read dense text, and with my high anxiety, sitting still to read felt impossible. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit in a folder on my laptop. The video content I watched was pretty minimal too—around 16 minutes total—because I preferred audio I could listen to while moving around.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it either directly impacted my grade or helped me connect with my peers. The peer interactions were genuinely valuable because they reduced my isolation and anxiety. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to assessment, I'd deprioritize it immediately. At 27, I don't have patience for activities that feel performative rather than educational.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford mentally. With my anxiety levels being what they are, I had to ruthlessly triage everything—required work barely got done, so optional stuff was out of the question. There was always this guilt about not doing more, but I had to protect my limited energy. Required activities carried the weight of consequences, which paradoxically made them both more stressful and more likely to get done.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded audio files and lecture recordings to my phone and listened during my commute and while cooking. I had a notebook where I'd jot down key concepts that I'd review away from screens. My peer support group also had a separate WhatsApp chat where we'd discuss course content informally. A lot of my actual learning happened in these spaces the LMS couldn't see.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was actively processing course material through conversations with my study group or re-listening to downloaded lectures, but my LMS activity showed almost nothing. I'd sometimes be lying in bed at night, unable to sleep from anxiety, mentally working through concepts from the course. The system only sees clicks and timestamps, not the thinking happening in between.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it three or four times over the following days without ever logging back in. My assignment scores were actually quite high—over 95%—partly because I'd deeply engage with materials offline before submitting. The LMS would show one brief access, but that single download represented hours of subsequent study.
Q12: My anxiety would spike periodically and make everything feel impossible. There were weeks where just opening my laptop felt like too much because I knew I'd see all the things I was behind on. When work got stressful or personal stuff happened, the course became this looming threat rather than something I was engaged with. My low resilience meant small setbacks could derail me for days.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by competing responsibilities, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. My task completion rate shows this—only about 43% despite logging in frequently. The fatigue and anxiety created this cycle where I'd feel too drained to engage properly, then feel more anxious about falling behind, then feel even more drained.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos or documents and my eyes would move across the content, but my brain was somewhere else entirely—usually spiraling about deadlines or life stuff. I'd reach the end of a page and realize I hadn't retained a single thing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to trick myself into feeling productive.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though I knew they were important—my completion rate was only about 17%. The anxiety made starting feel impossible; I'd build up the task in my mind until it seemed insurmountable. Then I'd avoid it, feel guilty, get more anxious, and avoid it more. It's a pattern I've struggled with for years, and knowing it's self-destructive doesn't make it easier to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. My engagement would spike dramatically in the 24-48 hours before something was due—my average response time of about 20 hours shows that last-minute pattern. The pressure of a deadline could temporarily override my avoidance tendencies, though it also massively increased my stress. Without deadlines, I'd drift indefinitely.
Q17: Positive feedback from peers in our support group would give me a temporary boost and make me want to engage more. When I did well on assignments, it validated that I could succeed despite my struggles. But feedback also triggered anxiety—I'd sometimes avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of disappointment. The peer support intervention helped because encouragement from classmates felt less judgmental than formal feedback.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules gave me a roadmap, but seeing all six online courses laid out made the workload feel crushing. I appreciated flexibility in when I could access materials since my schedule was unpredictable. The peer support component was genuinely the most influential structural element—it gave me human connection and accountability that kept me from completely disengaging.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because I could participate asynchronously and feel connected to others. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content across multiple courses—I was taking six simultaneously, which was probably too many given my mental state. The lack of audio-first options for some materials was frustrating since reading is harder for me. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped.
Q20: That describes me pretty well, actually. I'd log in frequently—47 times—but my assignment completion was low. For me, it was anxiety-driven checking without productive action. I'd look at what needed to be done, feel overwhelmed, maybe click around a bit, then close everything without actually completing tasks. High activity can mask avoidance behaviors and anxiety spirals that prevent real work from happening.
Q21: This also resonates with my experience in a different way. My exam score and assignment quality were decent when I actually submitted work, partly because of all the offline studying I did. Someone could watch minimal video content but deeply engage with downloaded materials elsewhere. The LMS doesn't capture listening to lectures on your phone or discussing concepts with peers outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click, submission timestamps. They're good at capturing frequency and timing of visible interactions. But they miss depth of engagement, offline study, emotional state, and the quality of learning happening. They can't tell if someone stared blankly at a video or was deeply focused. They definitely can't capture the anxiety that makes someone avoid logging in entirely.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and feeling connected to the learning community. It's not just completing tasks—it's wanting to learn, thinking about concepts outside of study time, and feeling like the course matters to your growth. For me, engagement fluctuated wildly based on my mental state. My happiest moments in this course were when peer discussions made me feel like I was part of something, not just struggling alone.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much the peer support intervention meant to me emotionally, or how my anxiety shaped every interaction with the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while walking to manage my stress, or the late-night conversations with study group members that helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show that despite my scattered engagement patterns, I ended up with a Merit and actually learned things that mattered to me. The numbers tell a story of inconsistency, but they miss the human struggling underneath.
